sc.d: You are now in charge of a struggling AM station. The ratings are soft. The morning host just quit. You have some money to turn it around (if you move quickly).

evq.d: FM stereo adoption accelerates.
Sun Belt growth surges. New ad dollars pour in.
Economic controls tighten. Ad budgets cut.
Automakers make FM standard. FM penetration accelerates.
Senate hearings dominate. News/Talk ratings spike.
Gas prices spike. Ad budgets slashed market-wide.
Stagflation hits. Ad revenue under sustained pressure.
Consumer confidence returns. Ad spending rebounds.
Disco explodes. Soul/R&B faces identity pressure.
FM audience exceeds AM for the first time. Permanent shift.
The soundtrack sells 40 million copies. Disco owns FM. Album Rock and Soul/R&B both feel the squeeze.
The FCC eliminates its format diversity requirements. Stations are free to chase any audience they want. The era of pure market logic begins.
Disco Demolition Night. The format collapses. Rock benefits.
Iranian Revolution. Gas spikes. Ad budgets cut.
Album Rock stations begin mining the catalog. New format unlocked.
Soft hits format dominates 25–49 demo.
Cable TV launches. Ad dollars begin diversifying.
Deep recession hits. Radio ad spending contracts.
Contemporary Hit Radio (CHR) — the evolved Top 40 lineage — tightens on FM: younger demos, higher stakes, louder format wars.
R&B-pop crossover explodes. Atlanta bonus format.
FM captures 70% of music listening. AM music stations face existential pressure.
AM stations discover that talk can thrive on personalities and older demos FM cannot easily reach.

evq.t: Adult Standards: Music of Your Life
evq.d: Gordon McLendon's syndicated format goes national — Sinatra, Ella, Nat King Cole on AM. A quiet audience nobody else wanted. Low shares, intensely loyal 55+ listeners. The format that never goes away.
Middle of the road (MOR) is dying on AM. FM ate the music audience. Survivors pivoting to News/Talk, Adult Contemporary, or Adult Standards.
Reagan boom. Ad spending surges.
’50s & 60s rock & roll becomes its own format. WCBS-FM pioneering. Boomer nostalgia is a business.
LA Olympics drives massive advertising demand.
Lush orchestral formats aging out fast. Stations pivoting to Soft AC or Adult Contemporary.
Upscale discovery rock for 25–54 FM listeners — a commercial lane distinct from public eclectic and from teen CHR.
The industry's last hope to save AM music. Listeners don't care. FM wins.
Market collapses. Ad budgets frozen at many companies.
A year after Black Monday, ad spending comes back stronger than expected. Radio benefits from TV's rising rates — local budgets flowing back to the dial.
Adult Contemporary has become the most-listened-to format in America. Stations targeting women 25-54 are printing money. The format war on FM is effectively over — until the next wave.
FM reaches near-saturation. AM music is functionally dead.
ESPN Radio launches. Sports Talk is now a real format.
24-hour coverage spikes talk radio ratings.
Nirvana changes everything. Alternative format emerges.
Congress is weighing whether to loosen local radio caps. Wall Street and broadcast groups are lobbying hard; independents warn of consolidation. Watch for FCC action within 12–18 months.
Gulf War recession. Ad market contracts.
Adult Standards solidifies as an AM refuge — Sinatra, Ella, Nat King Cole. Low shares, intensely loyal 55+ audience. The format that never goes away.
FCC allows 2 AM + 2 FM per market. Acquisition window opens.
Hispanic population growth. Spanish-language radio unlocked.
After the 1992 window, commissioners hint at another bump in AM/FM caps. Major group owners are preparing for another buying wave.
The last holdout music AMs reformat or go dark. FM is the only viable music signal.
Talk radio audience explodes. News/Talk and Sports Talk benefit.
FCC allows 3 AM + 3 FM per market.
Clinton boom begins. Ad spending surges.
The last Beautiful Music stations have converted. Adult Contemporary is the successor format.
Congress is finalizing a sweeping telecom bill. Radio ownership limits could change dramatically — industry lobbyists expect consolidation-friendly rules within months.
Middle of the road (MOR) is no longer viable as a commercial format. The format is dead.
Ownership caps lifted. Up to 8 stations per market. Consolidation begins.
97% of new cars have FM. A music AM in 1997 is a museum piece. Talk and all-news AMs with real newsrooms hold up better than music on the band — if the market can support the cost.
ClearWave and Veridian Media buying everything. Independents pressured.
ClearWave and Summit Broadcasting are buying stations faster than regulators can process the filings. Independent operators are getting offers they can't refuse. The era of local radio is ending.
Silicon Valley start-ups are buying radio time. The dot-com ad boom is starting to flood local markets. Revenue is climbing ahead of expectations.
Tech companies flooding radio with ad dollars. Revenue peaks.
Rhythmic CHR and Hot AC become dominant with younger demos.
Tech ad spending collapses. Radio revenue drops sharply.
News/Talk ratings spike dramatically.
Corporate groups roll out pre-recorded DJ tracks coast to coast. One personality now voices six markets. The live local DJ is becoming an endangered species.
Post-9/11 patriotism drives Country radio to record ratings. Toby Keith is everywhere. If you're in Country, this is your moment.
Another round of News/Talk ratings boost.
XM and Sirius pull premium listeners. Quiet drag begins.
Stations adding 70s & 80s, dropping 'Oldies' branding. The music is getting younger.
Pandora launches. Terrestrial revenue begins long decline.
Mid-decade boom. Ad spending growing.
The industry spent $500 million promoting HD Radio as the answer to satellite and streaming. Nobody bought the receivers. The last technical fix for terrestrial radio has failed.

Podcasts, streaming news apps, and satellite radio are eating into AM audiences. Pure AM Talk stations without FM simulcasts will face gradual share erosion through the 2010s. An FM simulcast or translator stops this immediately.
Satellite consolidates. Drag on terrestrial intensifies.
Worst recession since 1930s. Ad spending collapses.
Green shoots. Ad market begins cautious recovery.
PulseCast Radio app launches. Terrestrial content finds new distribution.
Pandora's IPO values the streaming service at $2.6 billion. For the first time, Wall Street is betting against terrestrial radio. The existential threat is now priced in.
AM Talk has become the loudest voice in American politics. Ratings are strong but the demo is aging and getting narrower. You're winning the argument and losing the audience.
Howard Stern–era shock talk and relationship shows prove FM can monetize host-driven spoken word outside hard news and sports.
The last pure Oldies stations have converted. Classic Hits is the successor — or the demo is 75+ and the format is dying.
Spotify growing fast. 18–34 demo drifting from terrestrial FM.
Digital ad spend growing. Radio holds share in local markets.

ClearWave (now PulseCast Group) and Tri-State Radio scramble to add FM simulcasts for their AM flagships. The AM signal quality gap vs. FM and streaming is now undeniable — even for dominant News/Talk and Sports stations.
Spotify crosses 40 million active users. The 18-34 demo now has a credible alternative to FM for music. Radio still wins in the car — for now.
Broadcast radio will generate $17.4 billion this year — the last time the industry posts growth before a multi-year slide. If you're not positioned now, you won't be.
Half of all 18–34s now stream more than they listen to radio.
Election year. News/Talk and local stations flooded with political spend.
Amazon Echo and Google Home put a radio in every kitchen — but also Spotify, podcasts, and Pandora. Radio's smart speaker presence is strong. The competition is stronger.
PulseCast Group warns of possible bankruptcy — massive debt left over from the ClearWave-era buyout wave. The consolidation era is eating itself. Debt-heavy group owners are cutting costs everywhere.
Streaming and on-demand audio nibble appointment listening. Personality talk with parasocial equity holds sellout a bit better.
Streaming has fundamentally reshaped radio economics.
Pandemic. Ad spending collapses overnight. Drive-time audiences vanish.
The campaign is complete.
